title: Athyrium subfluviale
intro: Athyrium subfluviale, also known as Mountain Lady fern, is a perennial fern species native to the mountainous regions of East Asia. The plant has been used in traditional medicine for its anti-inflammatory, antioxidant, and antimicrobial properties, providing potential health benefits such as wound healing, antiseptic properties, and relief from respiratory issues. The bioactive constituents of Athyrium subfluviale include flavonoids, saponins, and phenolic acids, which contribute to its medicinal properties. In traditional herbal medicine, Athyrium subfluviale is commonly prepared as a decoction or tea, and it is also used in the form of extracts or capsules in modern herbal supplements.
taxonomy: Athyrium subfluviale is a species of fern in the family Athyriaceae. This delicate fern is native to mountainous regions in East Asia. It belongs to the genus Athyrium, which consists of approximately 150 species of ferns. The classification of Athyrium subfluviale at the species level is further divided into the kingdom Plantae, and the phylum and class are both Pteridophyta and Polypodiopsida, respectively. The exact classification of this species may be subject to ongoing research and updates in the field of botany.
uses: Athyrium subfluviale, a species of lady fern native to North America, has been traditionally used in folk medicine for its medicinal properties. The plant contains flavonoids and sesquiterpenes, which are believed to have anti-inflammatory and antioxidant effects. Traditionally, Athyrium subfluviale was used to treat various health issues, including fever, rheumatism, and skin conditions. However, its efficacy and safety for human consumption have not been extensively scientifically studied. Further research is needed to fully understand the plant's potential medicinal uses.

side_effects: Athyrium subfluviale is a species of fern, and like many plants, it may cause allergic reactions in some individuals, such as skin irritation or respiratory issues.
